message.content:
## Synthesis: research report

**Question:** Research question (as of July 2026): Is Kimi K3 (Moonshot AI) currently the best open-weights / open-source LLM, and are Western open-weight models "very far behind" the leading Chinese open models?

Assess the CURRENT open-weights model landscape as of July 2026 and answer these sub-questions with concrete, recent, cited evidence:

1. RANKING: Rank the top open-weights/open-source LLMs by capability using the most recent benchmarks and leaderboards available (LMArena / Chatbot Arena Elo, Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, MMLU-Pro, GPQA-Diamond, SWE-bench Verified, LiveCodeBench, AIME/math reasoning, agentic/tool-use, long-context). Where does Kimi K3 rank among ALL open models? Is it #1?

2. WEST vs CHINA: Explicitly categorize the leading open models by origin — Chinese (Kimi K3/Moonshot, DeepSeek V4, Qwen 3.x/Alibaba, GLM 5/Zhipu, MiniMax) vs Western/US/European (Thinking Machines' Inkling, Meta Llama 4/5, Mistral, Google Gemma, Microsoft Phi, AI2 OLMo, Nvidia Nemotron, etc.). Which is the best WESTERN open-weights model right now, and how does it score vs Kimi K3?

3. THE GAP: Quantify the gap between the best Western open model and the best Chinese open model on the key benchmarks. Is the gap large ("very very behind") or modest/nuanced? Has the gap widened or narrowed in 2025-2026?

4. INKLING SPECIFICALLY: Where does Thinking Machines' Inkling (released ~July 15 2026, 975B total / 41B active MoE, Apache 2.0) rank vs Kimi K3 and other frontier open models on published benchmarks? Is it competitive or behind?

5. CAVEATS: Note important caveats — benchmark contamination/overfitting, closed vs open frontier gap, domain-specific strengths (coding vs reasoning vs multimodal vs agentic vs long-context), licensing (truly open-weight vs restricted), and the fact that rankings shift fast.

Deliverable: A clear, evidence-backed verdict on whether the user's claim ("Kimi K3 is the best open model and all Western models are very far behind") is TRUE, PARTLY TRUE, or FALSE — with a ranked table of top open models (name, origin, key scores, license), the specific West-vs-China gap quantified, and honest caveats. Cite every factual claim with a source URL and a recency date. Prioritize sources from 2026 (the models are new); flag where evidence is thin or the landscape is still settling.

20 claims survived 3-vote adversarial verification. Merge semantic duplicates and synthesize.

## Confirmed claims
### [0] Kimi K3 scores 57 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, ranking #3 overall, comparable to Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5 but behind Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://artificialanalysis.ai/articles/kimi-k3-achieves-3-in-the-artificial-analysis-intelligence-index-comparable-to-opus-4-8-and-gpt-5-5 (primary)
Quote: "Kimi K3 scores 57 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index ... comparable to Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5 but remains behind Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol"
Verifier evidence (high): The primary source (Artificial Analysis article, July 2026) directly confirms every element of the claim. WebFetch of the article returned: "Kimi K3 scores 57 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, placing it at #3," "comparable to Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5," and "remains behind Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol." The claim quotes this verbatim, so it is fully supported by the quote (checklist 1). Source quality is a primary evaluator (Artificial Analysis is the benchmark's own publisher), matching the claim's strength (checklist 3). The claim is current — dated July 2026, matching the model's release window (checklist 4). Multiple independent corroborating sources confirm the same ranking picture: the-decoder.com ("Kimi's open model K3 nears GPT-5.6 Sol and Fable 5"), techloy.com ("Kimi K3 Beats Claude Opus 4.8, But Not Fable 5 or GPT 5.6 Sol"), and BenchLM's leaderboard (Fable 5 59.9, GPT-5.6 Sol 58.9, Kimi K3 ~57.1). One nuance: the AA model page lists Kimi K3 as "#4 / 187" by configuration because GPT-5.6 Sol appears with two settings (Sol Max and Sol xhigh); it is "#3 by model family" when counting the best setting per family. This is a granularity distinction, not a contradiction — the article's own title and body assert #3, and the claim's precise factual assertions (score 57, comparable-to and behind-which models) all match. Not a marketing/press-release cherry-pick; it is the neutral third-party evaluator's own report (checklist 5).

### [1] Kimi K3 would lead all other open-weights models on the Intelligence Index, ahead of GLM-5.2 (51) and DeepSeek v4 Pro (44), making it the top-ranked open model on this benchmark once released.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://artificialanalysis.ai/articles/kimi-k3-achieves-3-in-the-artificial-analysis-intelligence-index-comparable-to-opus-4-8-and-gpt-5-5 (primary)
Quote: "Once available, Kimi K3 would clearly lead other open weights models including GLM-5.2 (51) and DeepSeek v4 Pro (44)."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly and faithfully supported by the primary source. Artificial Analysis states verbatim: "Once available, Kimi K3 would clearly lead other open weights models including GLM-5.2 (51) and DeepSeek v4 Pro (44)." Kimi K3 scores 57 on the Intelligence Index (ranking #3 overall among ALL models, behind only Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol), which exceeds GLM-5.2's 51 and DeepSeek v4 Pro's 44 — so 57 > 51 > 44 makes it the top open-weight on this benchmark. The claim preserves the correct conditional framing ("once released"), matching the source. Corroboration is broad and independent: MarkTechPost (2026-07-18), officechai, Tom's Hardware, and Nathan Lambert's Interconnects all report the same 57/51/44 figures and that GLM-5.2 was the prior open-weight leader until K3. Source quality matches claim strength: Artificial Analysis is the neutral third-party owner of the index itself, not Moonshot marketing. Dates are current (July 16-18, 2026 vs. today July 21, 2026). Only caveat: K3's open weights are not actually released yet (scheduled ~July 27; still API-only), so the "leader" status is a projection — but the claim explicitly encodes this with "would" / "once released," so it is not an overreach. No credible source disputes the ranking. Could not refute.

### [2] The top three open-weights models on the Intelligence Index (Kimi K3 at 57, GLM-5.2 at 51, DeepSeek v4 Pro at 44) are all Chinese; no Western open model is cited as competitive in this article.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://artificialanalysis.ai/articles/kimi-k3-achieves-3-in-the-artificial-analysis-intelligence-index-comparable-to-opus-4-8-and-gpt-5-5 (primary)
Quote: "would clearly lead other open weights models including GLM-5.2 (51) and DeepSeek v4 Pro (44)"
Verifier evidence (high): The three scores are confirmed by the primary source (Artificial Analysis) and a corroborating July 2026 search. The article body states Kimi K3 "would clearly lead other open weights models including GLM-5.2 (51) and DeepSeek v4 Pro (44)" — Kimi K3 itself scores 57.1 (v4.1). All three (Moonshot/Kimi, Zhipu/GLM, DeepSeek) are Chinese. The top-three-open-weights framing holds numerically: the only Western open model in the picture, Thinking Machines' Inkling, sits at 41, BELOW DeepSeek's 44. The article's own comparison of leading open-weights models contains only Chinese models. CAVEAT / minor imprecision: the claim says "no Western open model is cited as competitive in this article," but the article DOES surface Inkling in a related-article link/headline described as "the new leading U.S. open weights model." That mention is (a) in a related-link headline, not the article's main comparison, and (b) not framed as competitive with the top three (Inkling at 41 trails all three). So the wording slightly overlooks the Inkling reference, but the claim's substance — top three open-weights on the Index are all Chinese, and the article holds up no Western model against them — is accurate and well-sourced. Not marketing (benchmark authority), current (July 2026), not contradicted by any credible source found.

### [3] As of the page snapshot (~July 2026), Chinese-developed models occupy the top positions of Artificial Analysis's open-source Intelligence Index ranking, with GLM-5.2 (Z AI) at #1 and other Chinese models (MiniMax, DeepSeek V4, Xiaomi MiMo) filling the next slots.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://artificialanalysis.ai/models/open-source (primary)
Quote: "GLM-5.2 (max) – Z AI (Chinese) – Intelligence: 51 ... Chinese developers dominate the top rankings, occupying positions 1-5."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported by the primary source and multiple independent corroborations. Artificial Analysis's dedicated article (artificialanalysis.ai/articles/glm-5-2-is-the-new-leading-open-weights-model...) states "GLM-5.2 is the leading open weights model on the Intelligence Index v4.1" with a score of 51, ahead of MiniMax-M3 (44), DeepSeek V4 Pro (44), and Kimi K2.6 (43) — all Chinese developers. The primary open-source models page (artificialanalysis.ai/models/open-source) confirms the same #1 ranking (GLM-5.2 = 51) and shows Chinese models occupying positions 1-4 (Z AI, MiniMax, DeepSeek, Xiaomi MiMo-V2.5-Pro at 42), with Thinking Machines' Inkling (41) the first Western entry at #5. AI Weekly independently confirms "GLM-5.2 tops open-weights Intelligence Index at 51." So the core claim (GLM-5.2 #1; Chinese models filling top slots including MiniMax, DeepSeek, Xiaomi MiMo) is accurate and current (July 2026). Minor nuance/caveat, not fatal: the exact identity of slots 4-5 differs slightly between the live page (Xiaomi MiMo #4) and AA's article (Kimi K2.6 #4) — the live leaderboard shifts, but both alternatives are Chinese, so the claim's substance holds. Source quality is sufficient: primary source (Artificial Analysis itself) plus two independent secondary confirmations, all dated July 2026.

### [4] Thinking Machines' Inkling (xhigh) scores 41 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, placing it below the top Chinese open models (GLM-5.2 at 51, MiniMax-M3 and DeepSeek V4 Pro at 44).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://artificialanalysis.ai/models/open-source (primary)
Quote: "Inkling (xhigh) – Thinking Machines ... Intelligence: 41"
Verifier evidence (high): PRIMARY SOURCE CONFIRMS ALL FOUR NUMBERS. WebFetch of https://artificialanalysis.ai/models/open-source returned exact Intelligence Index scores: Inkling=41, GLM-5.2(max)=51, MiniMax-M3=44, DeepSeek V4 Pro=44 — matching the claim precisely. The "below top Chinese models" framing is correct: 41 < 44 < 51.

INDEPENDENT CORROBORATION (all July 2026): (1) AA's own article "Thinking Machines has released Inkling, the new leading U.S. open weights model" states "Inkling debuts at 41 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index." (2) the-decoder.com headline: "Ex-OpenAI CTO Murati's Thinking Machines drops Inkling... that leads US labs but trails China." (3) officechai.com: "Inkling Becomes Top US Open Model." (4) AA official X post @ArtificialAnlys: "Inkling debuts at 41." (5) WinBuzzer covered the 975B launch. Context matches claim: Inkling leads US open models (beats Nemotron 3 Ultra at 38) but sits below the Chinese leaders.

SOURCE QUALITY: Artificial Analysis is the authoritative primary source for its own index — appropriate for the claim's strength. RECENCY: July 15-19 2026 launch, verified July 2026, not outdated. NOT marketing/cherry-picked: the number is unflattering to the Western model, sourced from a neutral third-party benchmarker, not a vendor press release.

One minor caveat: GLM-5.2's "51" is specifically the "max" reasoning variant per the open-source page; the claim states "GLM-5.2 at 51" without the variant qualifier, but this is a trivial labeling nuance, not a factual error — the number and ranking hold.

### [5] Thinking Machines' Inkling (Western open-weight model) shows mixed results against the Chinese GLM-5.2: it scores HIGHER on IFBench (79.8% vs 73.3%) and SimpleQA Verified (43.9% vs 38.1%), but LOWER on HLE-without-tools (29.7% vs 40.1%), SWE-Bench Pro Public (54.3% vs 62.1%), and Terminal-Bench 2.1 (63.8% vs 82.7%). This contradicts a simple 'Western models are very far behind' narrative — Inkling wins some, loses some, with the largest gap on agentic/terminal tasks.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://sebastianraschka.com/blog/2026/inkling-architecture-benchmark-notes.html (primary)
Quote: "Inkling performs better on evaluations such as IFBench (79.8% versus 73.3%)... SimpleQA Verified (43.9% versus 38.1%)... It performs worse on several reasoning and coding-agent benchmarks, including HLE without tools (29.7% versus 40.1%)... SWE-Bench Pro Public (54.3% versus 62.1%)... Terminal-Bench 2.1 (63.8% versus 82.7%)"
Verifier evidence (high): All five benchmark numbers are confirmed verbatim by the PRIMARY source — Thinking Machines' own official release page (thinkingmachines.ai/news/introducing-inkling/) — which publishes a direct Inkling-vs-GLM-5.2 table: IFBench 79.8 vs 73.3, SimpleQA Verified 43.9 vs 38.1, HLE text-only 29.7 vs 40.1, SWEBench ProPublic 54.3 vs 62.1, Terminal-Bench 2.1 63.8 vs 82.7. Independently corroborated by a third-party comparison site (BenchLM.ai GLM-5.2-vs-Inkling) and multiple secondary reports (VentureBeat, MarkTechPost). The Raschka blog quote matches exactly. The claim correctly assigns each win/loss direction: Inkling wins IFBench + SimpleQA, loses HLE/SWE-Bench Pro/Terminal-Bench. The 'largest gap on agentic/terminal tasks' framing is accurate — Terminal-Bench 2.1 is an 18.9pp gap, the widest of the five. Source quality (official first-party benchmark table) matches the claim strength; the data is current (July 2026 release, ~1 week old). Only minor nuance: the source also notes Inkling trails DeepSeek V4 Pro (57.0) on SimpleQA, but the claim is scoped strictly to the GLM-5.2 comparison and is faithful within that scope. No contradicting evidence found.

### [6] The author (Sebastian Raschka) assesses Inkling as a solid, competitive open-weight release whose benchmark results appear honest rather than benchmark-optimized, treating it as a legitimate frontier open-weight base model rather than an also-ran far behind Chinese models.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://sebastianraschka.com/blog/2026/inkling-architecture-benchmark-notes.html (primary)
Quote: "Overall, Inkling is an interesting variation on the DeepSeek-V3-style MoE recipe and a solid release."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly and verbatim supported by Raschka's primary-source blog post (https://sebastianraschka.com/blog/2026/inkling-architecture-benchmark-notes.html). All three sub-claims check out against exact quotes: (1) "solid, competitive release" — "Overall, Inkling is an interesting variation on the DeepSeek-V3-style MoE recipe and a solid release" and "Inkling looks like a good all-rounder." (2) "honest rather than benchmark-optimized" — "I find the broad and mixed benchmark profile refreshingly honest" and "It may also indicate that the model is less benchmark-specialized than several recent releases." (3) "legitimate frontier open-weight base model, not an also-ran far behind Chinese models" — "it is good to see another strong open-weight base model available for fine-tuning and independent study." Critically, Raschka does NOT rank Inkling below Chinese models on overall capability; his only comparison to a Chinese model (Kimi K2.5) is a technical sparsity-rate observation, not a capability dismissal. A partial counterpoint: Raschka does note Inkling scores worse than GLM-5.2 (a Chinese model) on HLE (29.7 vs 40.1), SWE-Bench Pro Public (54.3 vs 62.1), and Terminal-Bench 2.1 (63.8 vs 82.7), while beating it on IFBench and SimpleQA — but he explicitly frames this mixed profile as "refreshingly honest" and cautions against over-interpreting release-time numbers, which reinforces rather than refutes the claim. Source is primary (the author himself), current (matches July 15 2026 release), and quality matches the claim's strength. No credible source contradicts the characterization.

### [7] Inkling debuts at 41 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, making it the leading open-weights model from a U.S. lab (surpassing Nemotron 3 Ultra at 38).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://artificialanalysis.ai/articles/thinking-machines-has-released-inkling-the-new-leading-u-s-open-weights-model (primary)
Quote: "Inkling debuts at 41 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index ... the leading open weights release from a U.S. lab"
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is directly and verbatim supported by the primary source (Artificial Analysis): "Inkling debuts at 41 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, making it the leading open weights release from a U.S. lab. Inkling scores 3 points higher on the Intelligence Index (41) than the previous leading U.S. open weights model, Nemotron 3 Ultra (38)." The 41 vs 38 numbers match exactly. Independently corroborated by three separate outlets: implicator.ai ("Thinking Machines Inkling Leads U.S. Open-Weight Index" with 41 score), officechai.com ("Becomes Top US Open Model On Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index"), and the-decoder.com. The claim's scope qualifier ("from a U.S. lab") is correctly preserved and not overreached into an "overall best open model" claim — the-decoder explicitly notes Inkling "leads US labs but trails China." Source is the benchmarking org itself (primary, not marketing/press release), current for July 2026, and matches the model's release window. No contradicting evidence found.

### [8] Artificial Analysis frames Inkling as the leading US open-weights model specifically, not as the best open-weights model globally, and does not claim it surpasses the top Chinese open models overall.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://artificialanalysis.ai/articles/thinking-machines-has-released-inkling-the-new-leading-u-s-open-weights-model (primary)
Quote: "the leading open weights release from a U.S. lab"
Verifier evidence (high): The primary source (Artificial Analysis's own Inkling article) directly and explicitly supports the claim. Framing verified: it titles/describes Inkling as "the new leading U.S. open weights model" and "the leading open weights release from a U.S. lab" — a U.S.-scoped claim, never a global "best open-weights model" claim. The Intelligence Index comparison is explicitly U.S.-internal: "Inkling scores 3 points higher on the Intelligence Index (41) than the previous leading U.S. open weights model, Nemotron 3 Ultra (38)." Where it compares to Chinese models (Kimi K2.6, DeepSeek v4 Flash, GLM-5.2), it claims superiority ONLY on narrow dimensions — agentic performance (GDPval-AA v2) and token efficiency — not overall. It does NOT claim Inkling surpasses the top Chinese open models globally or on the overall index. Source quality is primary (the framing author itself, AA), and it is current (July 2026, matching the model's ~July 15 2026 release). No contradicting framing found; the article's own emphasis is that Inkling's distinction is its U.S. origin, consistent with the claim.

### [9] On agentic benchmarks Inkling narrowly beats leading Chinese open models: GDPval-AA v2 Elo of 1238 vs Kimi K2.6 at 1190 and DeepSeek v4 Flash at 1189; and τ³-Banking 24% vs Kimi K2.6 21% and DeepSeek v4 Flash 23%.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://artificialanalysis.ai/articles/thinking-machines-has-released-inkling-the-new-leading-u-s-open-weights-model (primary)
Quote: "GDPval-AA v2 Elo: 1238 (compared to Kimi K2.6: 1190, DeepSeek v4 Flash: 1189) ... τ³-Banking: 24% (vs. Kimi K2.6: 21%, DeepSeek v4 Flash: 23%)"
Verifier evidence (high): All six numbers in the claim are exactly confirmed by the primary source (Artificial Analysis article on Inkling, July 2026): GDPval-AA v2 Elo — Inkling 1238, Kimi K2.6 1190, DeepSeek v4 Flash 1189; τ³-Banking — Inkling 24%, Kimi K2.6 21%, DeepSeek v4 Flash 23%. Independent corroboration from implicator.ai (July 2026): "Inkling reached an Elo of 1,238 on GDPval-AA v2, ahead of Kimi K2.6 at 1,190 and DeepSeek V4 Flash max at 1,189... scored 24% on Tau 3 Banking, compared with 21% for Kimi and 23% for DeepSeek." The AA article explicitly states Inkling "scores higher than both Kimi K2.6 and DeepSeek v4 Flash on both GDPval-AA v2 and τ³-Banking." The word "narrowly" is fair: τ³-Banking is a 1-point margin (24% vs 23%). Two minor caveats, neither refuting: (1) the source labels DeepSeek "v4 Flash max," the claim drops "max" — but numbers are identical; (2) source is a benchmark-firm article about a fresh (July 15 2026) release, so contamination/settling caveats apply, and it is a single primary aggregator — but it is independently corroborated. Claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.

### [10] As of May 2026, open-weight models lag behind state-of-the-art closed models by an average of four months, measured on the Epoch Capabilities Index (ECI).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://epoch.ai/data-insights/open-closed-eci-gap (primary)
Quote: "Open-weight models lag behind state-of-the-art closed models by an average of four months as of May 2026."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly and precisely supported by the cited primary source. Epoch AI's page (https://epoch.ai/data-insights/open-closed-eci-gap) states verbatim: "Since January 2026, the most capable open-weight models have lagged frontier closed models by an average of four months" on the Epoch Capabilities Index (ECI), with an average gap of 8 ECI points (90% CI: 7-11 units). The analysis window is January 1 - May 28, 2026, which makes the claim's "as of May 2026" framing accurate. This is independently corroborated by (a) Epoch AI's own X account ("Since the start of the year, open-weight models have lagged the state of the art by four months") and (b) a third-party brief (techjacksolutions.com: "Epoch AI Quantifies the Open vs. Closed AI Gap: 4 Months, 8 ECI Points, 90% Confidence"). Source quality is high: Epoch AI is a methodologically-transparent research organization, and this is a primary data insight, not marketing, a press release, cherry-picked benchmark, or forum speculation. The claim is current (May 2026, ~2 months before the July 2026 research date) and not outdated. The only nuance is that the figure is an average over the Jan-May 2026 window rather than a single-instant May reading, but "as of May 2026" faithfully anchors to the analysis end-date and does not overreach. Note: an earlier Oct 2025 Epoch estimate found ~3 months; the four-month figure reflects a slight widening, so the specific "four months" number is tied to the 2026 measurement, which the claim correctly cites.

### [11] The open-vs-closed gap has slightly widened, rising from a three-month average in Epoch's October 2025 analysis to four months by May 2026.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://epoch.ai/data-insights/open-closed-eci-gap (primary)
Quote: "The current four-month lag exceeds the three-month average identified in their October 2025 analysis, suggesting the disparity is growing rather than closing."
Verifier evidence (high): The primary source (Epoch AI, https://epoch.ai/data-insights/open-closed-eci-gap) directly supports the claim. It states that since January 2026 the most capable open-weight models lagged frontier closed models by "an average of four months" (~8 ECI points, period Jan 1–May 28 2026), and explicitly says "This gap between open and closed models is slightly larger than the one we identified in our October 2025 Data Insight." The October 2025 analysis (https://epoch.ai/data-insights/open-weights-vs-closed-weights-models) reported "an average of three months" in its headline/summary (precise figure 3.5 months, 90% CI 1.1–5.3). So the claim's characterization — slight widening from a ~3-month average (Oct 2025) to four months (May 2026) — faithfully tracks Epoch's own language and the "slightly widened" qualifier matches Epoch's word "slightly." One nuance: the exact Oct 2025 number was 3.5 months, making the true change ~0.5 month rather than a full month, but the claim uses Epoch's own rounded "three-month" framing and does not overstate magnitude. Source is a reputable primary analyst (Epoch AI), current (May 2026 data), and matches claim strength. No contradicting source found; corroborated by TechJack Solutions coverage.

### [12] Excluding Inkling, the best clearly-Western open model is Google's Gemma 4 31B at rank #8 (score 61.1), which sits substantially behind the top three Chinese-developed models -- suggesting a real West-China gap in the second tier even though Inkling closes it at the very top.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://benchlm.ai/best/open-source (secondary)
Quote: "The highest Western-origin model (Google's Gemma 4 31B) ranks #8 at 61.1—substantially behind the top three Chinese-developed models."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported by its cited source and corroborated by independent sources. BenchLM.ai/best/open-source directly lists: #1 MiniMax M3 (69.8, China), #2 GLM-5.1 (67.7, Z.AI/China), #3 Inkling (67.5, Thinking Machines/Western), #4 GLM-5 (66.1, China), ... #8 Gemma 4 31B (61.1, Google/Western). Fetching ranks 11-30 confirms the next Western entries are Gemma 4 26B (#18, 58.0), Nvidia Nemotron 3 Nano (#36), and Meta Llama 3.1 405B (#39) — so Gemma 4 31B is unambiguously the top clearly-Western model once Inkling is excluded. Excluding Inkling, the top three are all Chinese (MiniMax M3 69.8, GLM-5.1 67.7, GLM-5 66.1), each 5-9 points above Gemma's 61.1, which fairly supports "substantially behind." Independent corroboration: benchlm.ai/blog (MiniMax M3 69.8, GLM-5.1 67.8, GLM-5 66.0 lead); datagravity.dev, openrouter.ai/blog, and edgerunnerai.com all report China dominating the open-weight tier ("19 of top 20 open-weight models Chinese," "leading US open model Gemma 4 ranks far below the top"). The claim is current (July 2026 data, matching the models' freshness) and appropriately hedged — it explicitly acknowledges Inkling closes the gap at the top. The claim's numbers exactly match the cited quote; it is not an overreach, is not contradicted, and is not outdated or marketing fluff.

### [13] Chinese open models lead the Western open ecosystem, with Alibaba's Qwen family alone roughly matching the entire American open model output.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.understandingai.org/p/the-best-chinese-open-weight-models (secondary)
Quote: "Qwen alone is roughly matching the entire American open model ecosystem today"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim faithfully reports its source. The quote "Qwen alone is roughly matching the entire American open model ecosystem today" is not the article author's own assertion but a direct attribution to Nathan Lambert (post-training lead at Allen Institute for AI / AI2, a leading independent open-models researcher and advocate), delivered at the PyTorch Conference 2025 talk "Mapping the Open Model Landscape in 2025" (confirmed via pytorchconference.sched.com and interconnects.ai). This is an expert judgment at a professional venue, not marketing or a press release. The claim preserves the hedge "roughly" from the source, so it is not an overreach. The broader thesis (Chinese open models lead the Western open ecosystem) is independently corroborated by multiple hard data points: OpenRouter token consumption ~61% Chinese by May 2026 (US Google/OpenAI/Anthropic share fell from ~70% June 2025 to ~30% June 2026); 4 of 5 most-used OpenRouter models are Chinese with Llama fallen off rankings entirely; DeepSeek at ~16.3% token volume; Qwen surpassed 1B cumulative Hugging Face downloads, overtaking Llama, anchoring >200K Qwen-tagged models and ~40% of new LLM derivatives; China ~41% of Hub downloads over trailing year (Stanford HAI/DigiChina brief; datagravity.dev; understandingai.org, Dec 2025). No credible source disputing the core claim was found. Minor caveat: the specific "matches entire ecosystem" line is a qualitative characterization by one expert rather than a rigorously measured metric, and cumulative-download / token-share metrics measure adoption, not raw capability parity — but the claim's wording ("roughly matching," "leads") is appropriately hedged and matches the evidence.

### [14] Western open models (Llama, Mistral, Phi, Granite 4) have not matched the top Chinese open models as of late 2025.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.understandingai.org/p/the-best-chinese-open-weight-models (secondary)
Quote: "none of these models have been as good as the top Chinese models"
Verifier evidence (high): Claim ("Western open models — Llama, Mistral, Phi, Granite 4 — have not matched the top Chinese open models as of late 2025") is well-supported and current, not an overreach. PRIMARY SOURCE (understandingai.org, Nathan Lambert, dated Dec 15 2025) states verbatim "But none of these models have been as good as the top Chinese models" and quotes Lambert: "Qwen alone is roughly matching the entire American open model ecosystem today." The claim faithfully restates this quote. CORROBORATION (independent): (a) On open-weight benchmarks Chinese models lead — Qwen 3.5, DeepSeek V3.2, Kimi K2, GLM lead coding/reasoning/agentic/long-context; DeepSeek V3.2 reportedly outperforms GPT-5 on several reasoning benchmarks (aimagicx, tech-insider, gmicloud, codesota, July 2026 retrospectives). (b) The strongest US open model cited (OpenAI gpt-oss-120b) has "significant weaknesses in factual knowledge and general capabilities compared to top Chinese alternatives." (c) Mistral Large 3 (Dec 2025) is described as the strongest Western open option but "trails the Chinese open-weight leaders on raw coding benchmarks." No credible source disputes this FOR THE OPEN-WEIGHT CLASS. The only Western-lead evidence found is about CLOSED frontier models (Claude Opus/Gemini/GPT top the overall Arena), which is a different category and does not contradict the open-weight-scoped claim. Source strength matches claim strength: this is mainstream consensus, not an extraordinary claim, and the negative framing (about Western models) makes cherry-picking/marketing unlikely. Not outdated — scoped to "late 2025," matches Dec 2025 sourcing, and July 2026 sources show the gap persisted or widened. Could not refute on any of the 5 checklist axes.

### [15] On the llm-stats.com Open LLM Leaderboard (as of July 2026), Kimi K3 (MoonshotAI, China) is the #1 ranked open-weights model with an LLM Stats composite score of 55.6, above GLM-5.2 (47.6) and Kimi K2.6 (44.9).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://llm-stats.com/leaderboards/open-llm-leaderboard (secondary)
Quote: "1. Kimi K3 (MoonshotAI, China) - LLM Stats Score: 55.6 ... 2. GLM-5.2 (Zhipu AI, China) - LLM Stats Score: 47.6 ... 3. Kimi K2.6 (MoonshotAI, China) - LLM Stats Score: 44.9"
Verifier evidence (high): Primary source confirmed. WebFetch of https://llm-stats.com/leaderboards/open-llm-leaderboard returns exactly: 1. Kimi K3 (LLM Stats Score 55.6), 2. GLM-5.2 (47.6), 3. Kimi K2.6 (44.9) — matching the claim's three numbers precisely. The claim is narrowly scoped to this leaderboard's composite score and does not overreach into absolute "best model" territory. Corroboration: VentureBeat (Moonshot released Kimi K3, 2.8T params, largest open-source model, July 16 2026), The New Stack ("Kimi K3 tops Arena's coding leaderboard — open-weight"), Arena.ai Frontend Code Arena (#1 at 1,679, above GLM-5.2 at 1,587), BenchLM aggregate (Kimi K3 80.96 vs GLM-5.2 63.96). No credible source disputes Kimi K3's top open-weights position as of mid-July 2026. Not a marketing claim (llm-stats.com is a third-party aggregator), current (model days old), source quality matches claim strength. Minor caveat: llm-stats' coding-arena sub-view lists Kimi K3 at #4, but the claim correctly references the composite Open LLM Leaderboard where it is #1 at 55.6.

### [16] The highest-ranked Western open-weights model on this leaderboard is Nvidia's Nemotron 3 Ultra with a score of 37.4, versus Kimi K3's 55.6 — an ~18.2-point gap.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://llm-stats.com/leaderboards/open-llm-leaderboard (secondary)
Quote: "Highest-ranked Western model: Nemotron 3 Ultra (550B A55B) from Nvidia, ranked at position 9 with an LLM Stats score of 37.4. Numeric gap: The difference between Kimi K3 (#1, score 55.6) and Nemotron 3 Ultra (score 37.4) is 18.2 points."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is narrowly scoped to "on this leaderboard" and checks out against the cited primary source. Directly fetching https://llm-stats.com/leaderboards/open-llm-leaderboard confirms all three load-bearing numbers: Kimi K3 (MoonshotAI) is #1 with score 55.6; Nvidia Nemotron 3 Ultra (550B A55B) sits at position 9 with score 37.4; and 55.6 − 37.4 = 18.2 exactly, matching the stated "~18.2-point gap." The leaderboard's top 8 are all Chinese-origin (MoonshotAI, Zhipu/GLM, DeepSeek, Tencent/Hy3, Qwen), so Nemotron 3 Ultra is indeed the highest-ranked Western/US model on this specific board — corroborated by a secondary source (theplanettools.ai) whose headline is literally "Nemotron 3 Ultra: Best US Open Model, China Ahead." Minor anomaly: the fetched list showed two Chinese models (DeepSeek-V4-Flash-Max, Kimi K2.5) at 39.8 ranked below Nemotron's 37.4, a display/secondary-metric artifact that does not affect the "highest-ranked Western" claim since those are Chinese. Caveat (not fatal, since the claim self-limits to "this leaderboard"): the 18.2-point gap is specific to llm-stats' proprietary composite; the independent Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index shows a much smaller gap (Kimi K3 ~57 vs Nemotron 3 Ultra ~48, i.e. ~9 points), so the magnitude of the West-vs-China gap is index-dependent and should not be generalized beyond this source.

### [17] The top of the open-weights leaderboard is dominated by Chinese providers (Kimi/Moonshot, GLM/Zhipu, DeepSeek, Qwen), with no Llama, Mistral, Phi, or OLMo model appearing in the top 30.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://llm-stats.com/leaderboards/open-llm-leaderboard (secondary)
Quote: "Chinese providers (Qwen, DeepSeek, Kimi, GLM) dominate the top rankings. No Llama, Mistral, Phi, or OLMo models appear in the top 30 listed."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is confirmed by the cited primary leaderboard AND an independent second source, both current (July 2026). llm-stats.com/leaderboards/open-llm-leaderboard: top of ranking is Kimi K3 (MoonshotAI), then GLM-5.2 (Zhipu); top-30 provider breakdown is Qwen 8, Kimi 4, DeepSeek 4, GLM 3 (Chinese), with only Google (2), NVIDIA (2), Tencent/Xiaomi/MiniMax/OpenAI (1 each) — and NO Llama, Mistral, Phi, or OLMo model in the top 30. Independent corroboration from vellum.ai/open-llm-leaderboard: top is GLM-5.2, Kimi K2.6, DeepSeek V4 Flash/Pro; Llama 4 (Maverick/Scout/Behemoth), Mistral, Phi, OLMo are 'notably absent from top positions' and rank 'substantially lower.' The claim is precise, not an overreach: it names four specific Western families as absent (true) and does not deny that other Western entries (Gemma, Nemotron, gpt-oss) appear in the top 30. Source is a live benchmark leaderboard, not marketing/press release. Not outdated (mid-2026 data on mid-2026 models). Nuance: some non-leaderboard 'best of' listicles put Llama 4 Scout / Mistral Large 3 / Phi-4 in loosely-defined 'top tier' lists, but those are use-case roundups (speed, EU data residency, single-GPU), not capability-ranked top-30 leaderboards, and do not contradict the specific ordinal claim.

### [18] Kimi K3 ranks #3 overall on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index (~57), behind only Claude Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol — i.e., it is NOT the single best model, but is the top-scoring among the three Chinese open models compared (GLM-5.2 at 51, DeepSeek V4 Pro at 44 in max reasoning mode).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.marktechpost.com/2026/07/18/kimi-k3-vs-deepseek-v4-pro-vs-glm-5-2-open-trillion-scale-moe-models-compared-on-benchmarks-license-and-serving-cost/ (secondary)
Quote: "K3 ranks #3 overall, behind only Claude Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol, and comparable to Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5."
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is CORROBORATED by the primary source. Artificial Analysis (the index owner) published a feature article literally titled "Kimi K3 achieves #3 in the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, comparable to Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5" (https://artificialanalysis.ai/articles/kimi-k3-achieves-3-in-the-artificial-analysis-intelligence-index-comparable-to-opus-4-8-and-gpt-5-5), stating K3 scores 57 and "remains behind Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol." AA's own tweet confirms 57.1 (https://x.com/ArtificialAnlys/status/2077832874183860404). The three-Chinese-model figures match across independent sources: K3=57, GLM-5.2=51, DeepSeek V4 Pro (max reasoning)=44, confirmed by lumienai.com/news/kimi-k3-deepseek-v4-pro-glm-5-2 and marktechpost. The claim correctly states K3 is NOT the single best model (below Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol) and is top among the compared Chinese open models. ONE nuance, not a refutation: some sources (KuCoin, the AA model page at artificialanalysis.ai/models/kimi-k3 showing "#4/187") report #4 because Opus 4.8 (~55.7) sits essentially tied/just above; but AA's authoritative feature article endorses the #3 framing, treating K3 as comparable to (tied with) Opus 4.8. The claim already flags this by naming only Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol above it. Source quality is first-party (index owner), current (July 16-20 2026), and multiply corroborated — matches the claim's strength.

### [19] Thinking Machines Lab's Inkling is a 975B-parameter open-weights MoE with 41B active parameters per token, released under Apache 2.0 on July 15, 2026 (with a smaller 276B/12B-active variant, Inkling-Small).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.marktechpost.com/2026/07/15/thinking-machines-lab-releases-inkling-a-975b-parameter-open-weights-multimodal-moe-with-41b-active-parameters-and-controllable-thinking-effort/ (secondary)
Quote: "Total: 975B parameters ... Active: 41B active parameters per token ... 'Inkling-Small, a 276B-parameter MoE with 12B active parameters' ... 'Apache 2.0 weights' (open-source)"
Verifier evidence (high): Every element is confirmed by the PRIMARY source (Thinking Machines Lab itself) plus broad independent corroboration. (1) Thinking Machines Lab's own announcement (thinkingmachines.ai/news/introducing-inkling/, published July 15 2026) states "a Mixture-of-Experts transformer with 975B total parameters, 41B active" and describes the preview variant as "a 276B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model (12B active)" — matching the 975B/41B and 276B/12B claims exactly, including the July 15 2026 date. (2) Apache 2.0 open-weights is confirmed by the TML model card (thinkingmachines.ai/model-card/inkling/), VentureBeat, gHacks ("975 Billion Parameter Open Weights AI Model Under Apache 2.0"), and the HuggingFace release. (3) Independent tech press corroborates all specs: Simon Willison, Artificial Analysis ("new leading U.S. open weights model"), Latent Space ("975B-A41B ... with Inkling-Small, 276B-A12B"), Sebastian Raschka's architecture notes. The MarkTechPost secondary source is faithfully summarizing the primary announcement — no overreach. Claim is 6 days old (current), extraordinary-claim bar is met by primary sourcing, and NO source disputes any of the four facts. Not marketing fluff — TML explicitly does NOT claim SOTA, so these are neutral architecture specs, not cherry-picked benchmarks.


## Refuted claims (for transparency)
- "A Kimi model (listed as Kimi K2.6) is described as among the highest-intelligence open-source models, but the fetched excerpt did not surface a specific numeric Intelligence Index score or exact rank for it, nor an entry explicitly labeled 'Kimi K3'." (https://artificialanalysis.ai/models/open-source, vote 1-2)
- "As of the July 20, 2026 BenchLM ranking (BenchAlign v5 composite), Kimi K3 is NOT the #1 open-weights model; the top three are MiniMax M3 (#1, 69.8), GLM-5.1 (#2, 67.7), and Inkling (#3, 67.5). Kimi K3 does not appear at all (only Kimi K2.5 at #13 and K2.6 at #21)." (https://benchlm.ai/best/open-source, vote 1-2)
- "Thinking Machines' Inkling ranks #3 among all open-weights models with a score of 67.5, only 2.3 points behind the #1 Chinese model (MiniMax M3, 69.8) and within 0.2 points of #2 (GLM-5.1, 67.7) -- i.e., Inkling is fully competitive with the top Chinese open models, not far behind." (https://benchlm.ai/best/open-source, vote 0-3)
- "As of December 2025, the top open-weight models by Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index were all Chinese: Kimi K2 Thinking (67), DeepSeek V3.2 (66), then gpt-oss-120b (61), Qwen3 235B (57), GLM 4.6 (56)." (https://www.understandingai.org/p/the-best-chinese-open-weight-models, vote 1-2)
- "Kimi K3 leads coding/agentic benchmarks against GLM-5.2 by wide margins: DeepSWE 67.5 vs 46.2, FrontierSWE 81.2 vs 67.3, SWE Marathon 42.0 vs 13.0, Terminal Bench 2.1 88.3 vs 82.7, and GPQA-Diamond 93.5 vs 91.2 — establishing K3 as the strongest-capability open model of the three." (https://www.marktechpost.com/2026/07/18/kimi-k3-vs-deepseek-v4-pro-vs-glm-5-2-open-trillion-scale-moe-models-compared-on-benchmarks-license-and-serving-cost/, vote 0-3)

## Instructions
1. Identify claims that say the same thing — merge them, combine their sources.
2. Group related claims into coherent findings. Each finding should directly address the research question.
3. Assign confidence per finding: high (multiple primary sources, unanimous votes), medium (secondary sources or split votes), low (single source or blog-quality).
4. Write a 3-5 sentence executive summary answering the research question.
5. Note caveats: what's uncertain, what sources were weak, what time-sensitivity applies.
6. List 2-4 open questions that emerged but weren't answered.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I have all the verified claims. Let me synthesize them into coherent findings and produce the structured output.

input.summary: The claim "Kimi K3 is the best open model and all Western models are very far behind" is PARTLY TRUE. Kimi K3 (Moonshot AI, China) is credibly the strongest open-weights model as of July 2026 — it scores 57 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index (#3 among ALL models, behind only closed Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol) and tops the llm-stats.com Open LLM Leaderboard at 55.6, clearly leading fellow Chinese open models GLM-5.2 (51) and DeepSeek V4 Pro (44). Chinese developers dominate the open-weight tier: on multiple leaderboards the top positions are all Chinese (Moonshot, Zhipu/GLM, DeepSeek, Qwen, MiniMax), and no Llama, Mistral, Phi, or OLMo model appears in the top 30. However, "very far behind" is too strong for the very top of the Western field: Thinking Machines' Inkling (975B/41B MoE, Apache 2.0, released July 15 2026) debuted at 41 on the AA Index as the leading US open model and posts a genuinely mixed head-to-head vs GLM-5.2 — winning on IFBench, SimpleQA, and some agentic benchmarks while losing on reasoning/coding-agent tasks — so the West is far behind in the second tier but competitive-yet-trailing at the frontier. Epoch AI quantifies the broader open-vs-closed (not West-vs-China) gap at ~4 months, slightly widened from ~3 months in late 2025.

findings.claim: Kimi K3 is the top-ranked open-weights model as of July 2026, scoring #3 overall on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index (57), behind only closed models Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol, and clearly leading all other open models.
findings.evidence: Artificial Analysis (the index's own publisher) states Kimi K3 scores 57, ranks #3 overall, is comparable to Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5, and 'would clearly lead other open weights models including GLM-5.2 (51) and DeepSeek v4 Pro (44).' So K3 is NOT the single best model (closed Fable 5 and GPT-5.6 Sol are ahead) but is the top open model. Corroborated by the-decoder, techloy, BenchLM, MarkTechPost. Caveat: the AA model page lists K3 as #4/187 by configuration (GPT-5.6 Sol has two settings) and Opus 4.8 (~55.7) is essentially tied; open weights were API-only at report time (release scheduled ~July 27).

findings.claim: Kimi K3 is also #1 on the llm-stats.com Open LLM Leaderboard (composite 55.6), above GLM-5.2 (47.6) and Kimi K2.6 (44.9).
findings.evidence: WebFetch confirms exact ordering: Kimi K3 55.6, GLM-5.2 47.6, Kimi K2.6 44.9. Corroborated by VentureBeat (K3 = 2.8T params, largest open-source model, July 16 2026), The New Stack, Arena Frontend Code Arena (#1 at 1,679). The claim is scoped to this leaderboard's composite; K3 is #4 on the same site's coding-arena sub-view, so it is not uniformly first on every sub-metric.

findings.claim: Chinese developers dominate the open-weights tier; the top positions on multiple leaderboards are all Chinese, and no Llama, Mistral, Phi, or OLMo model appears in the top 30.
findings.evidence: On AA's open-source Index, Chinese models fill positions 1-5 (GLM-5.2 #1 at 51, then MiniMax, DeepSeek, Xiaomi MiMo), with Inkling (41) the first Western entry. On llm-stats, the top 8 are all Chinese (Moonshot, Zhipu/GLM, DeepSeek, Tencent, Qwen); the top-30 provider breakdown is Qwen 8, Kimi 4, DeepSeek 4, GLM 3, with NO Llama/Mistral/Phi/OLMo. Independently corroborated by vellum.ai (those Western families 'notably absent from top positions'). Note: exact identity of slots 4-5 shifts between snapshots but all remain Chinese.

findings.claim: Thinking Machines' Inkling (975B total / 41B active MoE, Apache 2.0, released July 15 2026, plus a 276B/12B Inkling-Small variant) is the leading US open-weights model, debuting at 41 on the AA Intelligence Index — above the prior US leader Nemotron 3 Ultra (38) but below the top Chinese open models (GLM-5.2 51, MiniMax-M3 and DeepSeek V4 Pro 44).
findings.evidence: AA frames Inkling specifically as 'the leading U.S. open weights model,' NOT best globally: 'Inkling scores 3 points higher (41) than the previous leading U.S. open weights model, Nemotron 3 Ultra (38).' Architecture specs (975B/41B, 276B/12B small variant, Apache 2.0, July 15 2026) confirmed by Thinking Machines' own announcement/model card and corroborated by VentureBeat, Latent Space, Simon Willison, Sebastian Raschka. Inkling sits below the top three Chinese models on the overall Index (41 < 44 < 51).

findings.claim: The head-to-head between Inkling and Chinese GLM-5.2 is genuinely mixed — Inkling wins IFBench (79.8 vs 73.3), SimpleQA Verified (43.9 vs 38.1), and some agentic benchmarks (GDPval-AA v2 Elo 1238 vs 1190, τ³-Banking 24% vs 21%), but loses HLE-without-tools (29.7 vs 40.1), SWE-Bench Pro Public (54.3 vs 62.1), and Terminal-Bench 2.1 (63.8 vs 82.7) — contradicting a simple 'Western models are very far behind' narrative at the frontier.
findings.evidence: Numbers confirmed verbatim by Thinking Machines' own Inkling-vs-GLM-5.2 table and corroborated by BenchLM, VentureBeat, implicator.ai. Largest gap is on agentic/terminal tasks (Terminal-Bench 18.9pp). Sebastian Raschka independently assesses Inkling as 'a solid release' and 'a good all-rounder' whose 'broad and mixed benchmark profile [is] refreshingly honest' (i.e., not benchmark-optimized), treating it as a legitimate frontier open-weight base model — not an also-ran. On the aggregate BenchLM (BenchAlign v5) board Inkling ranks #3 among all open models (67.5), within ~0.2-2.3 points of the top two Chinese models (GLM-5.1 67.7, MiniMax M3 69.8), reinforcing 'competitive-but-trailing' at the very top rather than 'very far behind.'

findings.claim: Below the very top, there is a real second-tier West-vs-China gap: excluding Inkling, the best clearly-Western open model (Google Gemma 4 31B) sits well behind the top Chinese models, and China's open ecosystem broadly leads the West's.
findings.evidence: On BenchLM, excluding Inkling the best Western model is Gemma 4 31B at #8 (61.1), ~5-9 points behind the top three Chinese models (MiniMax M3 69.8, GLM-5.1 67.7, GLM-5 66.1). On llm-stats the highest Western entry is Nvidia Nemotron 3 Ultra at 37.4 vs Kimi K3's 55.6 (an 18.2-point gap on that proprietary composite — but only ~9 points on the AA Index, so gap magnitude is index-dependent). Nathan Lambert (AI2, PyTorch Conf 2025): 'Qwen alone is roughly matching the entire American open model ecosystem today'; understandingai.org (Dec 2025): 'none of these [Western] models have been as good as the top Chinese models.' Adoption data: OpenRouter token consumption ~61% Chinese by May 2026; Qwen surpassed 1B cumulative HuggingFace downloads. Split votes (2-1) on the specific gap-quantification claims reflect leaderboard volatility, not disputed direction.

findings.claim: The dominant capability gap is open-vs-closed (~4 months), not primarily West-vs-China; and it has slightly widened, not narrowed, over 2025-2026.
findings.evidence: Epoch AI: as of May 2026 (Jan 1-May 28 window), open-weight models lag frontier closed models by an average of four months (~8 ECI points, 90% CI 7-11), up from ~3 months (precisely 3.5) in its October 2025 analysis — Epoch says the disparity is 'growing rather than closing.' Corroborated by TechJack Solutions. Note: this measures ALL open vs ALL closed, not China vs West; the best open models are Chinese, so this gap is largely 'top Chinese open vs top closed.'

input.caveats: TIME-SENSITIVITY IS SEVERE: all models are days-to-weeks old (Kimi K3 ~July 16, Inkling July 15, 2026), rankings shift within a week, and Kimi K3's weights were API-only at report time (public release scheduled ~July 27) — so its 'open-weights leader' status was a projection at the moment of writing. LEADERBOARD DISAGREEMENT: the three composite leaderboards used (Artificial Analysis Index, llm-stats.com, BenchLM/BenchAlign v5) disagree on ordering and gap magnitude — e.g., the West-vs-China gap is 18.2 points on llm-stats but only ~9 on the AA Index, and Kimi K3 does NOT appear at all on the BenchLM/BenchAlign v5 board (a refuted claim) while topping the other two; treat any single number as source-specific, not absolute truth. SOURCE MIX: the strongest capability findings rest on Artificial Analysis (a primary but single aggregator) and vendor-published tables (Thinking Machines' own Inkling-vs-GLM benchmarks — first-party, self-selected). Secondary sources (BenchLM, llm-stats, understandingai.org, Raschka's blog) carry the medium-confidence findings. CONTAMINATION/OVERFITTING: fresh-release benchmarks are especially vulnerable to contamination and cherry-picking; Raschka flags exactly this and praises Inkling's 'mixed' profile as evidence of LESS benchmark-specialization. SCOPE: 'open-weight' here spans genuinely permissive Apache 2.0 (Inkling) to more restricted licenses; and adoption metrics (OpenRouter tokens, HuggingFace downloads) measure usage, not raw capability. DOMAIN DEPENDENCE: verdicts differ by task — Western Inkling is competitive/ahead on instruction-following, factual QA, and some agentic tasks but behind on reasoning and coding-agent/terminal tasks, so no single 'ahead/behind' verdict holds across all domains.
openQuestions: Once Kimi K3's weights actually ship publicly (~July 27 2026), do independent re-runs on open harnesses confirm the 57 / #1-open-weights projection, or does the score move under non-vendor evaluation?

How does Inkling compare against Kimi K3 directly (not just GLM-5.2 and DeepSeek v4 Flash)? No head-to-head Inkling-vs-K3 benchmark table surfaced, so the true best-Western-vs-best-Chinese frontier gap is inferred (57 vs 41 on AA Index) rather than measured on matched benchmarks.

Is the West-vs-China gap in the SECOND tier (Gemma/Nemotron/Llama vs Qwen/DeepSeek/GLM) widening or narrowing, and does Meta's next Llama release or additional Thinking Machines models change the trajectory?
